one_liner: A 38-year-old male of Southeast Asian descent presents after a nocturnal syncopal episode with a family history of sudden cardiac death in a young male relative.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient's wife found him unresponsive in bed at 3 AM, making gasping sounds. She performed sternal rub and he regained consciousness after approximately 30 seconds. He has no memory of the event. Over the past year, he has had 2 episodes of near-syncope occurring at rest or during sleep. He denies exertional symptoms, chest pain, or palpitations. He had a fever 2 days ago (self-limited URI) and took acetaminophen. He has been otherwise healthy.
stage2.family_history: Brother died suddenly in sleep at age 32 (autopsy inconclusive — 'structurally normal heart'). Father is from the Philippines. Paternal uncle also died suddenly at age 35.
stage2.social_history: Born in the Philippines, moved to the US at age 10. Non-smoker. Social drinker (1-2 beers/week). Software engineer. Physically active. No illicit drugs.
stage2.physical_exam: General: Well-appearing male, alert and oriented, no acute distress. HEENT: Normal, no JVD. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, normal S1/S2, no murmurs, gallops, or rubs. Pulmonary: Clear bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. Extremities: No edema, normal pulses. Neurological: Alert, oriented, no focal deficits, no post-ictal features.

lab_results.result: Pathogenic variant identified in SCN5A gene
lab_results.clinical_significance: Confirms genetic basis of Brugada syndrome — SCN5A encodes the cardiac sodium channel

imaging_results.result: Normal sinus rhythm. Type 1 Brugada pattern: coved-type ST elevation ≥2 mm in leads V1-V2 with T-wave inversion. Normal QRS duration. Normal QTc.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Spontaneous Type 1 Brugada ECG pattern — diagnostic in the right clinical context

imaging_results.result: Structurally normal heart. LVEF 60%. No valvular disease. No RVOT abnormalities. Normal wall thickness.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Normal structural heart — Brugada is a channelopathy, not a structural disease

imaging_results.result: Normal biventricular size and function. No myocardial fibrosis or fatty infiltration. No ARVC features.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Excludes ARVC and other structural causes of sudden death

stage3.key_tests_rationale: ECG is the cornerstone of diagnosis showing the Type 1 Brugada pattern. Echo and MRI exclude structural heart disease. Electrolytes rule out metabolic causes. Genetic testing confirms the diagnosis and enables family screening.

stage5.final_clue: The ECG reveals a spontaneous Type 1 Brugada pattern (coved ST elevation in V1-V2) in a patient with aborted sudden cardiac death during sleep, a structurally normal heart, and family history of unexplained sudden death in young males of Southeast Asian descent. SCN5A mutation confirms Brugada syndrome.

debrief.classic_presentation: Brugada syndrome presents with syncope or sudden cardiac death, typically occurring at rest or during sleep, in a patient with a structurally normal heart. Mean age of presentation is 40 years with strong male predominance (8:1). It is most prevalent in Southeast Asian populations (known as 'sudden unexplained nocturnal death syndrome' or 'bangungut' in the Philippines). Fever can unmask or worsen the ECG pattern.
debrief.attending_mode_notes: Three ECG types exist but only Type 1 (coved pattern) is diagnostic. Type 2 (saddleback) and Type 3 require sodium channel blocker provocation (ajmaline or procainamide) to convert to Type 1 for diagnosis. Fever is a well-known trigger — aggressively treat fever in known Brugada patients. The EP study's role in risk stratification is debated. ICD is the only proven therapy to prevent SCD.
debrief.pathophysiology: Brugada syndrome results from loss-of-function mutations in SCN5A (or related genes), encoding the cardiac sodium channel Nav1.5. Reduced sodium current in the right ventricular epicardium creates a transmural voltage gradient, producing the characteristic ST elevation on ECG. This creates a substrate for phase-2 reentry, which can trigger polymorphic VT/VF. Vagal tone enhancement during sleep or rest lowers heart rate and increases the transmural gradient, explaining the nocturnal predilection.
debrief.treatment_overview: ICD implantation is the only proven treatment for prevention of SCD in symptomatic patients (survivors of cardiac arrest or syncope with Type 1 ECG). No antiarrhythmic drug reliably prevents VT/VF. Quinidine (Ito blocker) may be used as adjunctive therapy or when ICD is refused/contraindicated. Avoid all drugs that block sodium channels (Class I antiarrhythmics, tricyclic antidepressants, cocaine). Treat fever aggressively. Family screening with ECG and genetic testing. Refer to www.brugadadrugs.org for drug avoidance list.
high_yield_pearls: Only the Type 1 (coved) Brugada ECG pattern is diagnostic: ≥2 mm coved ST elevation in V1-V2 followed by T-wave inversion.
Brugada syndrome is the most common cause of sudden cardiac death in young individuals in Southeast Asia.
Fever can unmask or exacerbate the Brugada ECG pattern and trigger arrhythmias — antipyretics should be given promptly.
SCN5A mutations are found in only ~20-30% of Brugada patients — a negative genetic test does NOT exclude the diagnosis.
ICD is the only proven treatment for SCD prevention; isoproterenol is used acutely for electrical storm in Brugada patients.
sources: 2022 ESC Guidelines for Management of Ventricular Arrhythmias and Prevention of SCD
Brugada et al., Brugada Syndrome, Circulation Research 2017
Antzelevitch et al., Brugada Syndrome, JACC 2005
